title: ⚔️ Chapter Boss ⭐⭐⭐: Idioms and Figurative Language

questions.prompt:
Read: "After months of delays, the project team hit a brick wall when the supplier went bankrupt."
What does "hit a brick wall" mean in this context?

options.text: The team made rapid progress.
The team reached an obstacle that stopped their progress.
The team decided to change their supplier.
The team demolished the project's physical barriers.

questions.explanation: "Hit a brick wall" means to encounter an obstacle so significant that progress becomes impossible. The supplier going bankrupt is exactly that kind of obstacle. (a) is the opposite meaning; (c) is a possible next action but not what the idiom means; (d) is a literal misreading.

questions.prompt: Identify the METAPHOR in the following group of sentences.

options.text: The stock market crashed like a falling house of cards.
The negotiations were a battlefield from start to finish.
She was as bold as brass when she walked into the interview.
The office was as quiet as a library during the exam.

questions.explanation: "The negotiations were a battlefield" is a metaphor — it says one thing (negotiations) IS another (battlefield) without using like or as. (a) and (d) use as…as or like, making them similes. (c) as bold as brass is also a simile. Only (b) makes the implicit identification that defines a metaphor.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I'm still ___ about whether to accept the promotion — it means relocating my whole family."

options.text: blowing a fuse
spilling the beans
on the fence
turning over a new leaf

questions.explanation: "On the fence" means undecided — exactly the feeling described. "Blowing a fuse" = losing one's temper; "spilling the beans" = revealing a secret; "turning over a new leaf" = starting to behave better. None of these fit the meaning of being undecided about a job offer.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The job losses we've announced are only the tip of the iceberg. We've bitten the bullet on these cuts, but there is more to come."
Which interpretation is CORRECT?

options.text: The company made small cuts and is now eating better.
The visible job losses are small compared to what is coming, and the decision was painful but necessary.
The managers found the cuts enjoyable and easy to make.
The company decided not to make any more cuts after this announcement.

questions.explanation: "Tip of the iceberg" = the visible cuts are a small sign of something much larger to come. "Bitten the bullet" = endured the difficult decision bravely. Together they indicate the cuts were painful but necessary and more will follow. (a) and (c) are literal or inverted misreadings; (d) contradicts "there is more to come".

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an idiom INCORRECTLY (wrong form or wrong meaning in context)?

options.text: He's the spitting image of his father — they look identical.
She blew a fuse when she found out about the mistake.
We need to go the extra mile to satisfy this client.
They burned the bridge to save costs on the project.

questions.explanation: The error is (d): "burn bridges" (or "burn your bridges") means to irreparably destroy a relationship or opportunity — it cannot be used to describe saving costs on a project. The context is wrong and the form "burned the bridge" (singular) is also non-standard. (a), (b), and (c) all use their idioms correctly in context and in the right form.

questions.prompt:
Read: "After years of bad habits, he turned over a new leaf. He arrived at work on time at the drop of a hat when his boss asked."
Find the INCORRECTLY used idiom.

options.text: "turned over a new leaf" is used incorrectly.
Both idioms are used incorrectly.
"at the drop of a hat" is used incorrectly.
Both idioms are used correctly.

questions.explanation: "At the drop of a hat" means to do something immediately and without hesitation — it refers to spontaneous, unprompted action. But here, arriving on time because the boss asked is a prompted, expected response, not a spontaneous one; more importantly, the idiom awkwardly implies he only arrives on time when asked, contradicting the idea of genuine reform. "Turned over a new leaf" (b) is used correctly for changing habits. So (c) — the second idiom — is misapplied in context.
